questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: According to Mendel's Law of Segregation, what happens to the two alleles of a gene during gamete formation?

options.A: Both alleles pass into the same gamete
options.B: They segregate and each gamete receives only one allele
options.C: Alleles fuse before entering the gamete
options.D: Only the dominant allele passes into the gamete

questions.ncert_line: According to the Law of Segregation, the two alleles of a gene segregate during gamete formation and a gamete receives only one of the two factors.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 5 — Principles of Inheritance and Variation, Section 5.1: Mendel's Laws of Inheritance
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 5 — Principles of Inheritance and Variation, Section 5.1: Mendel's Laws of Inheritance

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In ABO blood group system, a person with genotype IAi will have which blood group?
questions.ncert_line: The three alleles IA, IB and i control the ABO blood groups. IA and IB are codominant and both are dominant over i. A person with genotype ii has blood group O.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 5 — Principles of Inheritance and Variation, Section 5.4: Codominance
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 5 — Principles of Inheritance and Variation, Section 5.4: Codominance

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Sickle cell anaemia is an example of which type of inheritance?
questions.ncert_line: Sickle cell anaemia is an autosome linked recessive trait. The disease is controlled by a single pair of alleles, HbA and HbS. Only homozygous individuals for HbS (HbS HbS) show the disease.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 5 — Principles of Inheritance and Variation, Section 5.6: Genetic Disorders
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 5 — Principles of Inheritance and Variation, Section 5.6: Genetic Disorders

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Down's syndrome results from trisomy of which chromosome?
questions.ncert_line: Trisomy of chromosome 21 causes Down's syndrome. The affected individual has 47 chromosomes. The chromosomal aberration is called trisomy. The features include a small round head, short stature, and mental retardation.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 5 — Principles of Inheritance and Variation, Section 5.6.2: Chromosomal Disorders
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 5 — Principles of Inheritance and Variation, Section 5.6.2: Chromosomal Disorders

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the orientation of the two strands of a DNA double helix?

options.A: Both strands run in the 5'→3' direction
options.B: Both strands run in the 3'→5' direction
options.C: The two strands are antiparallel (one 5'→3', the other 3'→5')
options.D: The orientation of the two strands is the same (parallel)

questions.ncert_line: James Watson and Francis Crick proposed the Double Helix model for the structure of DNA. The two strands of DNA are antiparallel. The bases of complementary strands are held together by hydrogen bonds.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 6 — Molecular Basis of Inheritance, Section 6.2: The DNA
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 6 — Molecular Basis of Inheritance, Section 6.2: The DNA

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a feature of DNA replication?

options.B: Each daughter DNA has one parental and one new strand
options.C: Both strands serve as templates simultaneously
options.D: It is conservative — the parental strands remain together

questions.ncert_line: DNA replication is semiconservative. The two strands of the parental DNA serve as template for the synthesis of the new complementary strands. Each daughter DNA molecule consists of one parental and one newly synthesised strand.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 6 — Molecular Basis of Inheritance, Section 6.4: DNA Replication
questions.explanation: Correct answer is D. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 6 — Molecular Basis of Inheritance, Section 6.4: DNA Replication

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: During transcription, which strand of DNA serves as the template?
questions.ncert_line: The strand that has the polarity 3'→5' acts as a template strand. The other strand with polarity 5'→3' is called the coding strand.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 6 — Molecular Basis of Inheritance, Section 6.5.1: Transcription Unit
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 6 — Molecular Basis of Inheritance, Section 6.5.1: Transcription Unit

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following codons does NOT code for any amino acid?
questions.ncert_line: The codon UGA is a stop codon (not coding for any amino acid). AUG is the start codon that codes for methionine. There are 61 sense codons and 3 stop codons (UAA, UAG, UGA).
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 6 — Molecular Basis of Inheritance, Section 6.5.4: Genetic Code
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 6 — Molecular Basis of Inheritance, Section 6.5.4: Genetic Code

questions.subject: Biology

questions.ncert_line: The codon UGA is a stop codon (not coding for any amino acid). AUG is the start codon that codes for methionine. There are 61 sense codons and 3 stop codons (UAA, UAG, UGA).
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 6 — Molecular Basis of Inheritance, Section 6.5.4: Genetic Code
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 6 — Molecular Basis of Inheritance, Section 6.5.4: Genetic Code

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In E. coli, the lac operon is switched ON when:

options.A: Glucose is present and lactose is absent
options.B: Both glucose and lactose are present in equal amounts
options.C: Lactose is present and binds to the repressor protein
options.D: The repressor binds to the promoter region

questions.ncert_line: In the absence of inducer (lactose), the repressor binds to the operator and blocks transcription. When lactose (inducer) is present, it binds to the repressor, inactivating it, and transcription proceeds.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 6 — Molecular Basis of Inheritance, Section 6.7: Regulation of Gene Expression
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 6 — Molecular Basis of Inheritance, Section 6.7: Regulation of Gene Expression

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What did Griffith call the chemical substance from heat-killed S-strain bacteria that could transform R-strain bacteria?
questions.ncert_line: Griffith's experiment showed that some chemical from the heat-killed virulent (S) bacteria could transform the non-virulent (R) bacteria into virulent bacteria. This principle was called the transforming principle.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 6 — Molecular Basis of Inheritance, Section 6.1.1: Griffith's Experiment
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 6 — Molecular Basis of Inheritance, Section 6.1.1: Griffith's Experiment

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In the Avery-MacLeod-McCarty experiment, transforming activity was destroyed only when treated with:
questions.ncert_line: Avery, MacLeod and McCarty demonstrated that DNA is the transforming principle. They used enzymes to destroy proteins (protease), RNA (RNase), and DNA (DNase) separately. Only DNase destroyed transforming activity.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 6 — Molecular Basis of Inheritance, Section 6.1.2: Search for the Genetic Material
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 6 — Molecular Basis of Inheritance, Section 6.1.2: Search for the Genetic Material

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In the Hershey-Chase experiment, radioactive 32P was used to label which component of bacteriophage T2?
questions.ncert_line: Hershey and Chase worked with T2 bacteriophage to prove that DNA is the genetic material. They grew some viruses on radioactive 35S (labels protein) and some on radioactive 32P (labels DNA).
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 6 — Molecular Basis of Inheritance, Section 6.1.3: Hershey and Chase Experiment
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 6 — Molecular Basis of Inheritance, Section 6.1.3: Hershey and Chase Experiment

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a condition for Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium?

options.C: High rate of natural selection favouring one allele
options.D: No migration of individuals into or out of the population

questions.ncert_line: Hardy-Weinberg principle states that allele frequencies in a population remain stable and constant from generation to generation — the gene pool remains constant. This is called genetic equilibrium.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 7 — Evolution, Section 7.6: Hardy-Weinberg Principle
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 7 — Evolution, Section 7.6: Hardy-Weinberg Principle

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Industrial melanism in Biston betularia (peppered moth) is an example of:

options.A: Mutation creating new dark alleles during industrialisation
options.B: Natural selection favouring pre-existing dark-coloured variants

questions.ncert_line: During the industrialisation period in England, the white-winged moths were eliminated by predators and melanised forms (darker forms) were selected. This is an example of natural selection.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 7 — Evolution, Section 7.4.1: Darwinian Fitness
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 7 — Evolution, Section 7.4.1: Darwinian Fitness

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In angiosperms, how many functional megaspores are typically produced from one megaspore mother cell?
questions.ncert_line: The megaspore mother cell (MMC) is a large cell with a dense cytoplasm. It undergoes meiosis to produce four megaspores. Usually only one megaspore is functional, the others degenerate.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 2 — Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants, Section 2.4.1: Megasporogenesis
questions.explanation: Correct answer is D. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 2 — Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants, Section 2.4.1: Megasporogenesis

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which cells undergo meiosis I during spermatogenesis?
questions.ncert_line: Spermatogenesis begins at puberty. The spermatogonia (2n) multiply by mitosis and grow into primary spermatocytes (2n). Each primary spermatocyte undergoes meiosis I to form secondary spermatocytes (n), then meiosis II to form spermatids.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 3 — Human Reproduction, Section 3.2.1: Spermatogenesis
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 3 — Human Reproduction, Section 3.2.1: Spermatogenesis

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which hormone surge triggers ovulation in the human menstrual cycle?
questions.ncert_line: The menstrual cycle consists of menstruation (days 1-5), the follicular/proliferative phase (days 6-13), ovulation (day 14), and the luteal/secretory phase (days 15-28). LH surge triggers ovulation.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 3 — Human Reproduction, Section 3.2.4: Menstrual Cycle
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 3 — Human Reproduction, Section 3.2.4: Menstrual Cycle

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The first menstruation beginning at puberty is called:
questions.ncert_line: The first menstruation begins at puberty and is called menarche. The cyclical menstruation continues till menopause.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 3 — Human Reproduction, Section 3.2.4: Menstrual Cycle
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 3 — Human Reproduction, Section 3.2.4: Menstrual Cycle

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The volume of air inspired or expired during a normal respiration (tidal volume) is approximately:
questions.ncert_line: Tidal Volume (TV) is the volume of air inspired or expired during normal respiration — about 500 mL. Inspiratory Reserve Volume (IRV) is about 2500-3000 mL. Expiratory Reserve Volume (ERV) is about 1000-1100 mL.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 17 — Breathing and Exchange of Gases, Section 17.3: Respiratory Volumes and Capacities
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. NCERT Reference: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 17 — Breathing and Exchange of Gases, Section 17.3: Respiratory Volumes and Capacities

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Vital capacity of lungs is the sum of:

options.B: Tidal volume + Inspiratory reserve volume + Expiratory reserve volume
options.D: Both B and C are correct

questions.ncert_line: Tidal Volume (TV) is the volume of air inspired or expired during normal respiration — about 500 mL. Inspiratory Reserve Volume (IRV) is about 2500-3000 mL. Expiratory Reserve Volume (ERV) is about 1000-1100 mL.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 17 — Breathing and Exchange of Gases, Section 17.3: Respiratory Volumes and Capacities
questions.explanation: Correct answer is D. NCERT Reference: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 17 — Breathing and Exchange of Gases, Section 17.3: Respiratory Volumes and Capacities

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which part of the nephron is impermeable to water but allows active transport of electrolytes?

options.A: Descending limb of loop of Henle
options.C: Ascending limb of loop of Henle

questions.ncert_line: The ascending limb of loop of Henle is impermeable to water but allows active transport of electrolytes. This creates the concentration gradient in the medulla. The descending limb is permeable to water.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 19 — Excretory Products and Their Elimination, Section 19.2.1: Tubular Reabsorption
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. NCERT Reference: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 19 — Excretory Products and Their Elimination, Section 19.2.1: Tubular Reabsorption

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: ADH (vasopressin) is synthesised in the hypothalamus but released from:
questions.ncert_line: ADH (antidiuretic hormone/vasopressin) and oxytocin are synthesised in the hypothalamus but stored and released from the neurohypophysis (posterior pituitary).
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 22 — Chemical Coordination and Integration, Section 22.2: Pituitary Gland
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. NCERT Reference: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 22 — Chemical Coordination and Integration, Section 22.2: Pituitary Gland

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which hormone stimulates the secretion of gastric juice (HCl and pepsinogen) from gastric glands?
questions.ncert_line: Gastrin stimulates the gastric glands to secrete HCl and pepsinogen. Secretin stimulates the pancreas to secrete bicarbonate ions. Cholecystokinin (CCK) causes the release of bile from the gall bladder.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 16 — Digestion and Absorption, Section 16.3.3: Hormones of GI Tract
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. NCERT Reference: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 16 — Digestion and Absorption, Section 16.3.3: Hormones of GI Tract

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The junction between two neurons across which nerve impulses are transmitted is called a:
questions.ncert_line: An impulse is transmitted from neuron to neuron across a synapse. At a chemical synapse, the electrical signal is converted to a chemical signal. Neurotransmitters are released from the presynaptic terminal into the synaptic cleft.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 21 — Neural Control and Coordination, Section 21.2.2: Synapse
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. NCERT Reference: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 21 — Neural Control and Coordination, Section 21.2.2: Synapse

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which structure in bacteria helps them attach to rocks and to host tissues?
questions.ncert_line: Prokaryotic cells lack membrane-bound organelles. The genetic material (DNA) is not enveloped by a nuclear membrane. The ribosome of prokaryotes is 70S (50S + 30S). Fimbriae help bacteria attach to rocks and host tissue.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 8 — Cell: The Unit of Life, Section 8.2: Prokaryotic Cells
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. NCERT Reference: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 8 — Cell: The Unit of Life, Section 8.2: Prokaryotic Cells

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following has 70S ribosomes?
questions.ncert_line: Ribosomes are the site of protein synthesis. In eukaryotes, the cytoplasmic ribosomes are 80S (60S + 40S). Mitochondria and chloroplasts contain 70S ribosomes (50S + 30S), similar to prokaryotic ribosomes.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 8 — Cell: The Unit of Life, Section 8.5.10: Ribosomes
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. NCERT Reference: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 8 — Cell: The Unit of Life, Section 8.5.10: Ribosomes

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: At which sub-stage of prophase I does crossing over (recombination) occur?
questions.ncert_line: Prophase I of meiosis is subdivided into leptotene, zygotene, pachytene, diplotene and diakinesis. Synapsis (pairing of homologous chromosomes) occurs during zygotene. Crossing over (recombination) occurs at pachytene.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 10 — Cell Cycle and Cell Division, Section 10.3.1: Meiosis I
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. NCERT Reference: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 10 — Cell Cycle and Cell Division, Section 10.3.1: Meiosis I

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Synapsis (pairing of homologous chromosomes with formation of synaptonemal complex) occurs at which sub-stage of prophase I?
questions.ncert_line: Prophase I of meiosis is subdivided into leptotene, zygotene, pachytene, diplotene and diakinesis. Synapsis (pairing of homologous chromosomes) occurs during zygotene. Crossing over (recombination) occurs at pachytene.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 10 — Cell Cycle and Cell Division, Section 10.3.1: Meiosis I
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. NCERT Reference: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 10 — Cell Cycle and Cell Division, Section 10.3.1: Meiosis I

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: DNA replication occurs during which phase of the cell cycle?
questions.ncert_line: Cell cycle consists of two phases: interphase (G1, S, G2) and M phase (mitosis). DNA synthesis occurs during the S phase. The G1 and G2 phases are gap phases involving intense metabolic activity.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 10 — Cell Cycle and Cell Division, Section 10.1: Cell Cycle
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. NCERT Reference: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 10 — Cell Cycle and Cell Division, Section 10.1: Cell Cycle

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: During which stage of mitosis do centromeres split and chromatids begin moving to opposite poles?
questions.ncert_line: Mitosis is divided into four stages: prophase, metaphase, anaphase and telophase. During anaphase, centromeres split and the chromatids move toward opposite poles pulled by spindle fibres.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 10 — Cell Cycle and Cell Division, Section 10.2: M Phase
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. NCERT Reference: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 10 — Cell Cycle and Cell Division, Section 10.2: M Phase

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which unit is used to express the productivity of an ecosystem?
questions.ncert_line: The rate of production of organic matter during photosynthesis is called Gross Primary Productivity (GPP). Net Primary Productivity (NPP) = GPP - Respiration. It is expressed in terms of g m-2 yr-1 or kcal m-2 yr-1.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 14 — Ecosystem, Section 14.2: Productivity
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 14 — Ecosystem, Section 14.2: Productivity

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Net Primary Productivity (NPP) of an ecosystem is:
questions.ncert_line: The rate of production of organic matter during photosynthesis is called Gross Primary Productivity (GPP). Net Primary Productivity (NPP) = GPP - Respiration. It is expressed in terms of g m-2 yr-1 or kcal m-2 yr-1.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 14 — Ecosystem, Section 14.2: Productivity
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 14 — Ecosystem, Section 14.2: Productivity

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is considered the most important cause driving species to extinction according to the 'Evil Quartet'?
questions.ncert_line: Habitat loss and fragmentation is the most important cause driving species to extinction. The other members of The Evil Quartet are: over-exploitation, alien species invasions, and co-extinctions.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 15 — Biodiversity and Conservation, Section 15.3: Loss of Biodiversity
questions.explanation: Correct answer is D. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 15 — Biodiversity and Conservation, Section 15.3: Loss of Biodiversity

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In which interaction does one organism benefit while the other is neither benefited nor harmed?
questions.ncert_line: In mutualism both species benefit (+/+). In commensalism one species benefits and the other is unaffected (+/0). In parasitism one benefits at the expense of the other (+/-). In competition both are negatively affected (-/-).
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 13 — Organisms and Populations, Section 13.3: Population Interactions
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 13 — Organisms and Populations, Section 13.3: Population Interactions

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: An epiphyte growing on a mango tree branch is an example of:
questions.ncert_line: In mutualism both species benefit (+/+). In commensalism one species benefits and the other is unaffected (+/0). In parasitism one benefits at the expense of the other (+/-). In competition both are negatively affected (-/-).
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 13 — Organisms and Populations, Section 13.3: Population Interactions
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 13 — Organisms and Populations, Section 13.3: Population Interactions

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following ecological pyramids is ALWAYS upright (never inverted)?
questions.ncert_line: A pyramid of numbers can be inverted (as in a tree ecosystem where one tree supports many insects). A pyramid of biomass can also be inverted (in an aquatic ecosystem). However, the pyramid of energy is always upright.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 14 — Ecosystem, Section 14.4: Ecological Pyramids
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 14 — Ecosystem, Section 14.4: Ecological Pyramids

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The reaction centre of Photosystem I has an absorption maxima at:
questions.ncert_line: In PS I, the reaction centre chlorophyll a has an absorption peak at 700 nm and is called P700. In PS II, the reaction centre chlorophyll a has an absorption peak at 680 nm and is called P680.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 13 — Photosynthesis in Higher Plants, Section 13.4: Light Reactions
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. NCERT Reference: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 13 — Photosynthesis in Higher Plants, Section 13.4: Light Reactions

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: P680, the reaction centre of PS II, absorbs light at 680 nm. It is called P680 because:

options.C: It absorbs maximally at 680 nm wavelength

questions.ncert_line: In PS I, the reaction centre chlorophyll a has an absorption peak at 700 nm and is called P700. In PS II, the reaction centre chlorophyll a has an absorption peak at 680 nm and is called P680.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 13 — Photosynthesis in Higher Plants, Section 13.4: Light Reactions
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. NCERT Reference: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 13 — Photosynthesis in Higher Plants, Section 13.4: Light Reactions

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which protein is considered the most abundant in the biosphere?
questions.ncert_line: The enzyme Rubisco (ribulose bisphosphate carboxylase-oxygenase) is the most abundant protein in the world. It catalyses the first step of carbon fixation by combining CO2 with RuBP.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 13 — Photosynthesis in Higher Plants, Section 13.5.1: Calvin Cycle
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. NCERT Reference: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 13 — Photosynthesis in Higher Plants, Section 13.5.1: Calvin Cycle

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which phytohormone is a gas at room temperature?
questions.ncert_line: Cytokinins promote cell division. Abscisic acid (ABA) promotes seed dormancy and stomatal closure. Ethylene is a gaseous plant hormone that promotes fruit ripening and abscission. Gibberellins promote stem elongation.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 15 — Plant Growth and Development, Section 15.4: Plant Growth Regulators
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. NCERT Reference: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 15 — Plant Growth and Development, Section 15.4: Plant Growth Regulators

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The technique used to amplify a specific segment of DNA in vitro is:
questions.ncert_line: Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) amplifies a specific segment of DNA. It involves denaturation (94°C), annealing of primers (~55°C), and extension by Taq polymerase (72°C). Each cycle doubles the amount of target DNA.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 11 — Biotechnology: Principles and Processes, Section 11.2.1: PCR
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 11 — Biotechnology: Principles and Processes, Section 11.2.1: PCR

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: EcoRI is a restriction endonuclease that recognises and cuts the sequence 5'GAATTC3'. What type of ends does it produce?
questions.ncert_line: Restriction endonucleases recognise specific palindromic nucleotide sequences in DNA and cut them. EcoRI recognises the sequence 5'GAATTC3' and cuts between G and A. They produce sticky ends which facilitate cloning.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 11 — Biotechnology: Principles and Processes, Section 11.1.1: Restriction Enzymes
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 11 — Biotechnology: Principles and Processes, Section 11.1.1: Restriction Enzymes

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Bt cotton is resistant to which type of pest?
questions.ncert_line: Bt toxin genes (cry genes) from Bacillus thuringiensis are incorporated into the cotton plant (Bt cotton). The cry genes encode Cry proteins (Bt toxin) which are toxic to lepidopteran insects (cotton bollworm).
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 12 — Biotechnology and Its Applications, Section 12.2.1: Bt Cotton
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 12 — Biotechnology and Its Applications, Section 12.2.1: Bt Cotton

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The first clinical gene therapy (1990) was given for deficiency of which enzyme?
questions.ncert_line: The first clinical gene therapy was given in 1990 to a 4-year old girl with adenosine deaminase (ADA) deficiency. Functional ADA cDNA was introduced into her lymphocytes. ADA deficiency can also be cured by bone marrow transplantation.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 12 — Biotechnology and Its Applications, Section 12.3: Gene Therapy
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 12 — Biotechnology and Its Applications, Section 12.3: Gene Therapy

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In which group of organisms are the cell walls made of two thin overlapping silica shells that fit together like a soap box?
questions.ncert_line: Chrysophytes (diatoms and golden algae) have cell walls made of two thin overlapping shells that fit together like a soap box. The cell walls are embedded with silica.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 2 — Biological Classification, Section 2.3: Kingdom Protista
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. NCERT Reference: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 2 — Biological Classification, Section 2.3: Kingdom Protista

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question:
Assertion (A): All vertebrates are chordates but all chordates are not vertebrates.
Reason (R): Vertebrates possess a notochord only during the embryonic period, which is later replaced by a cartilaginous or bony vertebral column.

options.A: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
options.B: Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true but R is false
options.D: A is false but R is true

questions.ncert_line: The notochord is replaced by a cartilaginous or bony vertebral column in the adult vertebrate. Thus all vertebrates are chordates but all chordates are not vertebrates.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.2: Phylum Chordata
questions.explanation: Correct answer is A. NCERT Reference: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.2: Phylum Chordata

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Gemmae are structures found in bryophytes that help in:
questions.ncert_line: In bryophytes, the main plant body is a gametophyte (haploid). The sporophyte is dependent on the gametophyte for nutrition. Gemmae are green multicellular asexual buds found in gemma cups, helping in vegetative reproduction.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 3 — Plant Kingdom, Section 3.2: Bryophytes
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. NCERT Reference: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 3 — Plant Kingdom, Section 3.2: Bryophytes

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In which of the following plants do the male and female gametophytes NOT have an independent free-living existence?
questions.ncert_line: In gymnosperms, the male and female gametophytes do not have an independent free-living existence. They are totally dependent on the sporophyte. Cedrus is an example of gymnosperm.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 3 — Plant Kingdom, Section 3.4: Gymnosperms
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. NCERT Reference: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 3 — Plant Kingdom, Section 3.4: Gymnosperms

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Immature lymphocytes differentiate into antigen-sensitive lymphocytes in:

options.B: Primary lymphoid organs (thymus and bone marrow)

questions.ncert_line: The thymus and bone marrow are the primary lymphoid organs where immature lymphocytes differentiate into antigen-sensitive lymphocytes. Secondary lymphoid organs include the spleen, lymph nodes, and tonsils.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 8 — Human Health and Disease, Section 8.2.1: Immune System
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 8 — Human Health and Disease, Section 8.2.1: Immune System

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Who proposed the five kingdom classification of living organisms?
questions.ncert_line: R.H. Whittaker proposed the five kingdom classification: Monera, Protista, Fungi, Plantae and Animalia. The basis of classification included: cell structure, body organisation, mode of nutrition, reproduction and phylogenetic relationships.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 2 — Biological Classification, Section 2.1: Kingdom Monera
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 2 — Biological Classification, Section 2.1: Kingdom Monera

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Mycoplasmas are unique among bacteria because they:
questions.ncert_line: Mycoplasmas are organisms that completely lack a cell wall. They are the smallest living cells known. They can survive without oxygen and are pathogenic in animals and plants.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 2 — Biological Classification, Section 2.2.4: Mycoplasma
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 2 — Biological Classification, Section 2.2.4: Mycoplasma

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question:
Assertion (A): Lichens are considered good indicators of air pollution.
Reason (R): Lichens are highly sensitive to SO₂ and other air pollutants and cannot survive in polluted air.

options.A: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
options.B: Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true but R is false
options.D: A is false but R is true

questions.ncert_line: Lichens are symbiotic associations between algae (phycobiont) and fungi (mycobiont). The algal component is autotrophic and provides food; the fungal component provides shelter, absorbs water and minerals. Lichens are good pollution indicators.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 2 — Biological Classification, Section 2.4.5: Lichens
questions.explanation: Correct: A. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 2 — Biological Classification, Section 2.4.5: Lichens

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Prions differ from viroids in that prions:

options.A: Have single-stranded RNA as their genome
options.B: Are infectious protein particles with no nucleic acid
options.D: Require a host cell nucleus to replicate

questions.ncert_line: Viroids are free infectious RNA molecules without a protein coat, smaller than viruses. They cause diseases in plants. Prions are infectious protein particles (with no nucleic acid) that cause diseases like BSE (mad cow disease) and Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease in humans.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 2 — Biological Classification, Section 2.6.2: Viroids and Prions
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 2 — Biological Classification, Section 2.6.2: Viroids and Prions

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The cell wall of fungi is composed of:
questions.ncert_line: Fungi have cell walls made of chitin. They are heterotrophic (saprophytic, parasitic or symbiotic). The vegetative body is made of hyphae forming a mycelium. Asexual reproduction occurs by conidia, sporangiospores or budding.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 2 — Biological Classification, Section 2.4: Kingdom Fungi
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 2 — Biological Classification, Section 2.4: Kingdom Fungi

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following correctly matches the algal class with its stored food?
questions.ncert_line: Chlorophyceae (green algae) have chlorophyll a and b, starch as stored food, and flagella of equal length (isokont). Phaeophyceae (brown algae) have chlorophyll a, c, fucoxanthin, and mannitol/laminarin as stored food. Rhodophyceae (red algae) have r-phycoerythrin and floridean starch.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 3 — Plant Kingdom, Section 3.1: Algae
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 3 — Plant Kingdom, Section 3.1: Algae

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Pteridophytes are significant in plant evolution because they were the first plants to have:
questions.ncert_line: Pteridophytes are the first terrestrial plants with vascular tissue (xylem and phloem). The main plant body is a sporophyte (diploid). Examples: Selaginella (heterosporous), Equisetum, Pteris, Nephrolepis. The gametophyte (prothallus) is small, inconspicuous and independent.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 3 — Plant Kingdom, Section 3.3: Pteridophytes
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 3 — Plant Kingdom, Section 3.3: Pteridophytes

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Animals that possess a true coelom (body cavity lined by mesoderm on all sides) are called:
questions.ncert_line: The basis of classification of animals includes: levels of organisation (cellular, tissue, organ), symmetry (radial, bilateral), coelom (acoelomate, pseudocoelomate, coelomate), segmentation, notochord, and germinal layers (diploblastic, triploblastic).
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.1: Basis of Classification
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.1: Basis of Classification

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The 'collar cells' (choanocytes) in sponges are responsible for:

options.C: Creating water current and capturing food

questions.ncert_line: Porifera (sponges) are diploblastic (two germinal layers), with a cellular level of organisation. They have a water canal system. Choanocytes (collar cells) line the spongocoel and canals. Body is supported by spicules or spongin fibres.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.2.1: Phylum Porifera
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.2.1: Phylum Porifera

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Cnidoblasts (nematocysts) present in Coelenterata are used for:
questions.ncert_line: Coelenterata (Cnidaria) are diploblastic with radial symmetry and tissue level of organisation. They have cnidoblasts (nematocytes) with stinging cells. They show alternation of generations between polyp (asexual) and medusa (sexual) forms.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.2.2: Phylum Coelenterata
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.2.2: Phylum Coelenterata

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In Platyhelminthes, the excretion and osmoregulation is carried out by:
questions.ncert_line: Platyhelminthes (flatworms) are triploblastic, acoelomate with bilateral symmetry and organ level of organisation. They are mostly endoparasites — Taenia (tapeworm), Fasciola (liver fluke). The flame cells help in osmoregulation and excretion.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.2.3: Phylum Platyhelminthes
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.2.3: Phylum Platyhelminthes

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The excretory organs in Annelida are:
questions.ncert_line: Annelida are triploblastic, coelomate, bilaterally symmetrical and metamerically segmented. Excretion by nephridia. Earthworm (Lumbricus) is hermaphrodite. Polychaetes have parapodia for locomotion. Hirudinaria is a bloodsucking leech.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.2.6: Phylum Annelida
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.2.6: Phylum Annelida

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic feature of Arthropoda?
questions.ncert_line: Arthropoda is the largest phylum with jointed appendages and exoskeleton of chitin. They have an open circulatory system. Excretion by Malpighian tubules (insects) or green glands (crustaceans). Examples: Locusta (grasshopper), Limulus (king crab), Apis (honeybee).
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.2.7: Phylum Arthropoda
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.2.7: Phylum Arthropoda

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The unique locomotory and feeding structure in Echinoderms is the:

options.B: Tube feet (part of water vascular system)

questions.ncert_line: Echinoderms are triploblastic, coelomate with radial symmetry (in adults) but bilateral symmetry in larvae. They have a unique water vascular system for locomotion. They have power of regeneration. Examples: Asterias (starfish), Echinus (sea urchin), Ophiura (brittle star).
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.2.10: Phylum Echinodermata
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.2.10: Phylum Echinodermata

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which tissue provides mechanical strength to plants and has dead cells at maturity?
questions.ncert_line: Meristematic tissues (apical, lateral, intercalary) have the ability to divide. Permanent tissues are either simple (parenchyma, collenchyma, sclerenchyma) or complex (xylem, phloem). Sclerenchyma provides mechanical strength — cells are dead at maturity.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 6 — Anatomy of Flowering Plants, Section 6.1: Plant Tissues
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 6 — Anatomy of Flowering Plants, Section 6.1: Plant Tissues

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following components of phloem is LIVING at maturity?
questions.ncert_line: Xylem consists of tracheids, vessels, xylem fibres and xylem parenchyma. Vessels are dead at maturity and help in water conduction. Phloem consists of sieve tube elements, companion cells, phloem fibres and phloem parenchyma. Sieve tube elements are living.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 6 — Anatomy of Flowering Plants, Section 6.1.3: Complex Tissues
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 6 — Anatomy of Flowering Plants, Section 6.1.3: Complex Tissues

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Casparian strips in the endodermis of roots are made of:
questions.ncert_line: The transverse section of a dicot root shows: epidermis (with root hairs), cortex, endodermis (with Casparian strips), pericycle, vascular bundles (xylem and phloem alternating, exarch). Pericycle gives rise to lateral roots.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 6 — Anatomy of Flowering Plants, Section 6.2: Anatomy of Dicotyledonous Root
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 6 — Anatomy of Flowering Plants, Section 6.2: Anatomy of Dicotyledonous Root

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The cork cambium (phellogen) produces cork (phellem) toward the outside and what toward the inside?
questions.ncert_line: Secondary growth in dicot stems is brought about by two lateral meristems: vascular cambium (produces secondary xylem/wood and secondary phloem) and cork cambium (phellogen, produces cork/phellem outward and phelloderm inward).
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 6 — Anatomy of Flowering Plants, Section 6.3: Secondary Growth
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 6 — Anatomy of Flowering Plants, Section 6.3: Secondary Growth

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In a racemose inflorescence, the flowers are borne in:
questions.ncert_line: The arrangement of flowers on the floral axis is called inflorescence. In a racemose inflorescence, the main axis grows continuously and flowers are borne laterally (acropetal succession). In a cymose inflorescence, the main axis terminates in a flower.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 5 — Morphology of Flowering Plants, Section 5.4: The Inflorescence
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 5 — Morphology of Flowering Plants, Section 5.4: The Inflorescence

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In a typical flower, the androecium consists of:

options.C: Stamens, each with an anther and a filament

questions.ncert_line: A complete flower has all four whorls: calyx (sepals), corolla (petals), androecium (stamens) and gynoecium (pistil/carpels). The androecium consists of stamens (anther + filament). The gynoecium consists of one or more carpels (stigma + style + ovary).
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 5 — Morphology of Flowering Plants, Section 5.5: The Flower
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 5 — Morphology of Flowering Plants, Section 5.5: The Flower

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which type of muscle is involuntary, striated, and branched?
questions.ncert_line: The four types of animal tissues are: epithelial, connective, muscular and neural. Epithelial tissue forms the covering of body surfaces. Connective tissues have cells scattered in a matrix. Cardiac muscle is involuntary, striated and branched.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 7 — Structural Organisation in Animals, Section 7.1: Animal Tissues
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 7 — Structural Organisation in Animals, Section 7.1: Animal Tissues

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: According to the fluid mosaic model, the plasma membrane consists of:

options.A: A lipid bilayer with protein molecules embedded in it
options.B: A protein bilayer with lipid molecules embedded in it
options.C: A single layer of lipids with proteins on the outside only
options.D: Equal amounts of carbohydrates and proteins

questions.ncert_line: The fluid mosaic model (Singer and Nicolson, 1972) describes the plasma membrane as a fluid bilayer of phospholipids with proteins floating in it. Cholesterol is present between phospholipids and helps in maintaining membrane fluidity.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 8 — Cell: The Unit of Life, Section 8.3: Plasma Membrane
questions.explanation: Correct: A. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 8 — Cell: The Unit of Life, Section 8.3: Plasma Membrane

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Lysosomes are called 'suicidal bags' of the cell because:

options.A: They produce ATP for cell death
options.B: They contain hydrolytic enzymes that can digest cellular contents
options.D: They release toxins to kill pathogens

questions.ncert_line: Lysosomes are membrane-bound vesicles containing hydrolytic enzymes (acid hydrolases). They digest foreign material (heterophagy) and worn-out organelles (autophagy). They are formed by the Golgi apparatus. They are called the 'suicidal bags' of the cell.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 8 — Cell: The Unit of Life, Section 8.5.6: Lysosomes
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 8 — Cell: The Unit of Life, Section 8.5.6: Lysosomes

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The dark reactions (Calvin cycle) of photosynthesis occur in which part of the chloroplast?
questions.ncert_line: Chloroplasts are double-membrane organelles. The inner membrane is less permeable. The stroma contains thylakoids arranged in stacks (grana). Light reactions occur on the thylakoid membranes; dark reactions (Calvin cycle) occur in the stroma. Chloroplasts have their own DNA.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 8 — Cell: The Unit of Life, Section 8.5.8: Plastids
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 8 — Cell: The Unit of Life, Section 8.5.8: Plastids

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The nucleolus disappears during cell division. Its primary function in interphase is:
questions.ncert_line: The nucleus has a double membrane called the nuclear envelope with nuclear pores. The nucleoplasm contains chromatin (DNA + proteins). The nucleolus is the site of ribosomal RNA synthesis and ribosome assembly. It disappears during cell division.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 8 — Cell: The Unit of Life, Section 8.5.11: Nucleus
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 8 — Cell: The Unit of Life, Section 8.5.11: Nucleus

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is a free-living nitrogen-fixing bacterium found in soil?
questions.ncert_line: Biological nitrogen fixation is carried out by: free-living bacteria (Azotobacter, Clostridium), symbiotic bacteria (Rhizobium in root nodules of legumes), and cyanobacteria (Anabaena, Nostoc). The enzyme nitrogenase catalyses N₂ → NH₃. It requires strict anaerobic conditions.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 12 — Mineral Nutrition, Section 12.4: Nitrogen Cycle
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 12 — Mineral Nutrition, Section 12.4: Nitrogen Cycle

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The enzyme responsible for biological nitrogen fixation is:
questions.ncert_line: Biological nitrogen fixation is carried out by: free-living bacteria (Azotobacter, Clostridium), symbiotic bacteria (Rhizobium in root nodules of legumes), and cyanobacteria (Anabaena, Nostoc). The enzyme nitrogenase catalyses N₂ → NH₃. It requires strict anaerobic conditions.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 12 — Mineral Nutrition, Section 12.4: Nitrogen Cycle
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 12 — Mineral Nutrition, Section 12.4: Nitrogen Cycle

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Chlorosis (yellowing) appearing first in young leaves is typically caused by deficiency of:

options.A: Nitrogen (mobile — appears in old leaves first)
options.B: Potassium (mobile — appears in old leaves first)
options.C: Iron (immobile — appears in young leaves first)
options.D: Magnesium (mobile — appears in old leaves first)

questions.ncert_line: Iron (Fe) deficiency causes chlorosis (yellowing) in young leaves. Calcium (Ca) deficiency causes death of growing tips (necrosis of meristems). Potassium (K) deficiency causes chlorosis in older leaves first. Boron (B) deficiency causes death of terminal buds.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 12 — Mineral Nutrition, Section 12.3: Deficiency Symptoms of Essential Elements
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 12 — Mineral Nutrition, Section 12.3: Deficiency Symptoms of Essential Elements

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The cohesion-tension theory of ascent of sap was proposed by:
questions.ncert_line: The cohesion-tension theory (Dixon and Joly) explains ascent of sap. Water molecules are held together by cohesion forces and to cell walls by adhesion. Transpiration from leaves creates tension (negative pressure) that pulls water up through xylem.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 11 — Transport in Plants, Section 11.4.2: Ascent of Sap
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 11 — Transport in Plants, Section 11.4.2: Ascent of Sap

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question:
Assertion (A): Transport in phloem is bidirectional (can move up or down).
Reason (R): Phloem transports sugars from source (leaf) to sink (roots/fruits) according to the pressure flow hypothesis.

options.A: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
options.B: Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true but R is false
options.D: A is false but R is true

questions.ncert_line: The pressure flow hypothesis (Ernst Münch) explains phloem transport. High sugar concentration at source (leaf) creates high osmotic pressure; water moves in → high turgor pressure → pushes sap toward sink (root/fruit). The flow is bidirectional in phloem.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 11 — Transport in Plants, Section 11.4.3: Phloem Transport
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 11 — Transport in Plants, Section 11.4.3: Phloem Transport

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Short day plants flower when the duration of darkness exceeds a critical period. The photoreceptor involved in this response is:
questions.ncert_line: Photoperiodism is the response of plants to the relative duration of light and darkness. Short day plants (SDP) flower when the night period exceeds a critical length. Long day plants (LDP) flower when the night period is below the critical length. The photoreceptor is phytochrome.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 15 — Plant Growth and Development, Section 15.5: Photoperiodism
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 15 — Plant Growth and Development, Section 15.5: Photoperiodism

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Vernalisation refers to the promotion of flowering by:
questions.ncert_line: Vernalisation is the acceleration of flowering by a period of low temperature. It is the cold treatment requirement for flowering in some plants (e.g., winter wheat, biennial plants like sugar beet). It prevents precocious reproductive development.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 15 — Plant Growth and Development, Section 15.6: Vernalisation
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 15 — Plant Growth and Development, Section 15.6: Vernalisation

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The net yield of ATP per glucose molecule in glycolysis is:
questions.ncert_line: Glycolysis occurs in the cytoplasm and produces 2 pyruvate, 2 ATP (net), and 2 NADH from one glucose molecule. Under aerobic conditions, pyruvate enters the mitochondria. The Krebs cycle produces 3 NADH, 1 FADH₂, 1 GTP per acetyl-CoA turn.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 14 — Respiration in Plants, Section 14.2–14.4
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 14 — Respiration in Plants, Section 14.2–14.4

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The respiratory quotient (RQ) for the complete oxidation of glucose (carbohydrate) is:
questions.ncert_line: Respiratory quotient (RQ) = CO₂ released / O₂ consumed. For carbohydrates, RQ = 1. For fats, RQ < 1 (about 0.7) as fats are more reduced and need more O₂. For organic acids, RQ > 1. RQ for succulent plants undergoing CAM is ≈ 0 at night.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 14 — Respiration in Plants, Section 14.6: Respiratory Quotient
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 14 — Respiration in Plants, Section 14.6: Respiratory Quotient

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following does NOT cause stomatal closure?
questions.ncert_line: Stomata open when guard cells become turgid and close when they become flaccid. Stomatal opening is triggered by: light, low CO₂ concentration, and K⁺ accumulation in guard cells. ABA (abscisic acid) promotes stomatal closure.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 11 — Transport in Plants, Section 11.3.3: Stomatal Transpiration
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 11 — Transport in Plants, Section 11.3.3: Stomatal Transpiration

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which enzyme converts starch to maltose in the mouth?
questions.ncert_line: Salivary amylase (ptyalin) digests starch to maltose. Pepsin in stomach digests proteins. Trypsin (from pancreas) digests proteins. Lipase (from pancreas) digests fats. Lactase digests lactose. The intestinal juice contains maltase, sucrase, lactase and peptidases.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 16 — Digestion and Absorption, Section 16.2–16.3
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 16 — Digestion and Absorption, Section 16.2–16.3

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: After absorption in intestinal cells, fats are transported via chylomicrons into the:
questions.ncert_line: Glucose and amino acids are absorbed by active transport. Fatty acids and glycerol are absorbed by diffusion and re-synthesised into lipoproteins (chylomicrons) in intestinal cells. Fats enter the lymph (lacteals) via chylomicrons, NOT directly into the blood.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 16 — Digestion and Absorption, Section 16.4: Absorption of Digested Products
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 16 — Digestion and Absorption, Section 16.4: Absorption of Digested Products

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The normal RBC count in human blood is approximately:
questions.ncert_line: Blood is composed of plasma (55%) and formed elements (45%). Plasma contains water (90-92%), proteins (fibrinogen, globulins, albumins). RBCs (erythrocytes) contain haemoglobin. Normal RBC count: 5 million/mm³. Platelets (thrombocytes) help in clotting.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 18 — Body Fluids and Circulation, Section 18.1: Blood
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 18 — Body Fluids and Circulation, Section 18.1: Blood

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The natural pacemaker of the human heart is the:
questions.ncert_line: The human heart has four chambers: right atrium, right ventricle, left atrium, left ventricle. The right side pumps deoxygenated blood to the lungs (pulmonary circuit). The left side pumps oxygenated blood to the body (systemic circuit). SA node is the pacemaker.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 18 — Body Fluids and Circulation, Section 18.3: Heart
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 18 — Body Fluids and Circulation, Section 18.3: Heart

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following distinguishes lymph from blood plasma?

options.C: Lymph has fewer proteins and no RBCs

questions.ncert_line: Lymph is a colourless fluid similar to plasma but with less proteins and no RBCs. The lymph nodes are present along lymphatic vessels. Lymph returns proteins and fluids to the blood. It carries chylomicrons (fat) absorbed from the intestine into the blood.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 18 — Body Fluids and Circulation, Section 18.6: Lymphatic System
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 18 — Body Fluids and Circulation, Section 18.6: Lymphatic System

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What percentage of the 180 litres of glomerular filtrate formed daily is reabsorbed back into the blood?
questions.ncert_line: Urine formation involves three processes: ultrafiltration (at Bowman's capsule), selective reabsorption (along tubules), and tubular secretion. About 180 litres of filtrate forms per day, but only 1-2 litres of urine is excreted (99% reabsorption). ADH promotes water reabsorption.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 19 — Excretory Products, Section 19.2: Mechanism of Urine Formation
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 19 — Excretory Products, Section 19.2: Mechanism of Urine Formation

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The juxtaglomerular apparatus (JGA) secretes renin in response to:
questions.ncert_line: The juxtaglomerular apparatus (JGA) is a specialised structure formed by the macula densa (modified cells of DCT) and juxtaglomerular cells (modified smooth muscle cells of afferent arteriole). JGA secretes renin when blood pressure falls, activating the renin-angiotensin system.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 19 — Excretory Products, Section 19.4: Regulation of Kidney Function
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 19 — Excretory Products, Section 19.4: Regulation of Kidney Function

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: According to the sliding filament theory, muscle contraction is initiated when calcium ions bind to:
questions.ncert_line: According to the sliding filament theory, muscle contraction occurs when actin filaments slide over myosin filaments. Cross bridges form between myosin heads and actin. ATP hydrolysis provides energy. Calcium ions (released from SR) initiate contraction by binding to troponin.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 20 — Locomotion and Movement, Section 20.2.2: Mechanism of Muscle Contraction
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 20 — Locomotion and Movement, Section 20.2.2: Mechanism of Muscle Contraction

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The total number of bones in the adult human skeleton is:
questions.ncert_line: The human skeleton has 206 bones. The axial skeleton (80 bones) includes skull, vertebral column, sternum and ribs. The appendicular skeleton (126 bones) includes limbs and girdles. Joints can be fibrous (immovable), cartilaginous (slightly movable) or synovial (freely movable).
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 20 — Locomotion and Movement, Section 20.3: Skeletal System
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 20 — Locomotion and Movement, Section 20.3: Skeletal System

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which division of the autonomic nervous system is responsible for the 'fight or flight' response?
questions.ncert_line: The central nervous system (CNS) consists of the brain and spinal cord. The peripheral nervous system (PNS) consists of cranial and spinal nerves. The autonomic nervous system (ANS) has sympathetic (fight or flight) and parasympathetic (rest and digest) divisions.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 21 — Neural Control, Section 21.4: Central Neural System
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 21 — Neural Control, Section 21.4: Central Neural System

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The semicircular canals in the inner ear are primarily responsible for:

options.D: Converting sound waves to nerve impulses

questions.ncert_line: The human ear has three parts: outer ear (pinna and auditory canal), middle ear (tympanic membrane, malleus, incus, stapes), and inner ear (cochlea and vestibular apparatus). The cochlea is for hearing; semicircular canals are for balance.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 21 — Neural Control, Section 21.3.2: Ear
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 21 — Neural Control, Section 21.3.2: Ear

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which microorganism is used in the commercial production of acetic acid (vinegar)?
questions.ncert_line: Lactobacillus and other lactic acid bacteria (LAB) help in curd formation — they convert lactose to lactic acid. Baker's yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae) is used in bread making. Aspergillus niger produces citric acid. Acetobacter aceti produces acetic acid (vinegar).
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.1: Microbes in Household Products
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.1: Microbes in Household Products

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Penicillin was discovered by Alexander Fleming from the fungus:
questions.ncert_line: Penicillin was discovered by Alexander Fleming from Penicillium notatum (now P. chrysogenum). It was the first antibiotic. Streptomycin is produced by Streptomyces griseus. Chloramphenicol is produced by Streptomyces venezuelae.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.2: Microbes in Industrial Products
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.2: Microbes in Industrial Products

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand) is used to measure:

options.A: Amount of dissolved oxygen in water
options.B: Degree of organic pollution in water

questions.ncert_line: Primary treatment of sewage removes floating and suspended solid matter by filtration and sedimentation. Secondary (biological) treatment uses microbes to break down organic matter — the BOD (biochemical oxygen demand) is reduced. Activated sludge contains aerobic microbes.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.5: Microbes in Sewage Treatment
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.5: Microbes in Sewage Treatment

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Baculoviruses are used as biocontrol agents because they are:

options.B: Species-specific pathogens that spare other organisms

questions.ncert_line: Baculoviruses (Nuclear Polyhedrosis Viruses — NPV) are species-specific pathogens used as biocontrol agents against insects and arthropods. Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) is a biocontrol agent against butterfly caterpillars. Trichoderma is used to control plant fungal pathogens.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.3: Microbes in Biocontrol
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.3: Microbes in Biocontrol

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The main component of biogas produced in a biogas plant is:
questions.ncert_line: Biogas (mainly methane) is produced by anaerobic bacteria (methanogens) from biomass. Methanobacterium is a methanogenic archaebacterium found in rumen of cattle. Biogas plants decompose cattle dung (gobar gas) anaerobically to produce biogas.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.4: Microbes as Biofuel
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.4: Microbes as Biofuel

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is essential in a cloning vector for identifying successful recombinants?
questions.ncert_line: A cloning vector must have: an origin of replication (ori), a selectable marker (antibiotic resistance gene), and cloning sites (restriction enzyme recognition sites). Plasmids, bacteriophages (λ phage) and cosmids are commonly used vectors.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 11 — Biotechnology: Principles and Processes, Section 11.1.2: Cloning Vectors
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 11 — Biotechnology: Principles and Processes, Section 11.1.2: Cloning Vectors

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The first recombinant human insulin (Humulin) was produced commercially in the bacterium:
questions.ncert_line: Recombinant human insulin (Humulin) was the first commercially produced recombinant protein. It was produced in E. coli. The insulin gene was produced from two separate chains (A and B) with C-peptide linking them. Eli Lilly produced it commercially in 1982.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 12 — Biotechnology and Its Applications, Section 12.2.2: Genetically Engineered Insulin
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 12 — Biotechnology and Its Applications, Section 12.2.2: Genetically Engineered Insulin

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: RNA interference (RNAi) involves gene silencing using:
questions.ncert_line: RNA interference (RNAi) is a mechanism of gene silencing using dsRNA (double-stranded RNA). Nematode-resistant tobacco was developed using RNAi. The dsRNA initiates RNAi in host cells, preventing translation of specific mRNA, providing protection against nematodes.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 12 — Biotechnology and Its Applications, Section 12.2.3: RNA Interference
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 12 — Biotechnology and Its Applications, Section 12.2.3: RNA Interference

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Agrobacterium tumefaciens is used in plant genetic engineering because:

options.A: It can fix nitrogen in plant cells
options.B: Its Ti plasmid can transfer T-DNA into the plant genome
options.C: It produces antibiotics that select for transformed cells
options.D: It has restriction enzymes compatible with plant DNA

questions.ncert_line: Agrobacterium tumefaciens is a soil bacterium that causes crown gall disease in plants. Its Ti (tumour-inducing) plasmid can integrate a segment of its DNA (T-DNA) into the plant genome. It is used as a vector to introduce foreign genes into plant cells.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 11 — Biotechnology: Principles and Processes, Section 11.1.5: Agrobacterium as Vector
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 11 — Biotechnology: Principles and Processes, Section 11.1.5: Agrobacterium as Vector

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The final step in decomposition that releases inorganic nutrients from humus is called:
questions.ncert_line: Decomposition is carried out by bacteria and fungi (decomposers) on dead organic matter (detritus). The steps are: fragmentation (by detritivores), leaching, catabolism, humification (dark-coloured humus formation) and mineralisation.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 14 — Ecosystem, Section 14.3: Decomposition
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 14 — Ecosystem, Section 14.3: Decomposition

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The final stable community that develops at the end of ecological succession is called the:
questions.ncert_line: Primary succession begins in bare areas (bare rock, pond, sand dune). The pioneer community modifies the environment making it suitable for more complex communities. Each successive community is called a seral community. The final stable community is the climax community.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 14 — Ecosystem, Section 14.6: Ecological Succession
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 14 — Ecosystem, Section 14.6: Ecological Succession

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: India has three biodiversity hotspots. Which of the following is NOT one of them?
questions.ncert_line: Biodiversity hotspots are regions with high levels of endemic species and high levels of habitat loss. India has three hotspots: the Western Ghats and Sri Lanka, the Eastern Himalayas, and the Indo-Burma region.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 15 — Biodiversity and Conservation, Section 15.4.2: Hotspots
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 15 — Biodiversity and Conservation, Section 15.4.2: Hotspots

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is an ex situ conservation method?
questions.ncert_line: In situ conservation means conserving species in their natural habitats (national parks, wildlife sanctuaries, biosphere reserves). Ex situ conservation is off-site conservation in zoos, botanical gardens, seed banks and cryopreservation. Seed banks preserve seeds at −196°C (liquid nitrogen).
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 15 — Biodiversity and Conservation, Section 15.4: Conservation of Biodiversity
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 15 — Biodiversity and Conservation, Section 15.4: Conservation of Biodiversity

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In the logistic growth model, the population growth rate is maximum when the population size N equals:
questions.ncert_line: Population growth can be exponential (J-shaped, dN/dt = rN) or logistic (S-shaped, dN/dt = rN(K−N)/K). The logistic growth has a carrying capacity K. The inflection point of logistic growth is at N = K/2 where growth rate is maximum.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 13 — Organisms and Populations, Section 13.2.1: Growth Models
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 13 — Organisms and Populations, Section 13.2.1: Growth Models

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The international agreement to phase out CFC production to protect the ozone layer is called the:
questions.ncert_line: Ozone (O₃) in the stratosphere absorbs harmful UV-B radiation. CFCs (chlorofluorocarbons) released from refrigerants and aerosols destroy the ozone layer. The ozone hole is most prominent over Antarctica. The Montreal Protocol (1987) banned CFCs.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 16 — Environmental Issues, Section 16.5: Ozone Depletion
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 16 — Environmental Issues, Section 16.5: Ozone Depletion

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Eutrophication refers to:

options.A: Depletion of oxygen in water bodies
options.B: Excessive nutrient enrichment causing algal bloom in water bodies
options.C: Accumulation of heavy metals in water

questions.ncert_line: The full form of BOD is Biochemical Oxygen Demand — it is the amount of oxygen needed by bacteria to decompose organic matter in water. High BOD indicates high organic pollution. Eutrophication is excessive growth of phytoplankton due to enrichment of nutrients in water bodies.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 16 — Environmental Issues, Section 16.2: Water Pollution
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 16 — Environmental Issues, Section 16.2: Water Pollution

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which nutrient cycle does NOT have a significant atmospheric component?
questions.ncert_line: In the carbon cycle, carbon enters living systems through photosynthesis and returns to the atmosphere through respiration, decomposition and combustion. Phosphorus cycle has no atmospheric component — it cycles from soil to organisms and back. Nitrogen cycle involves fixation, nitrification, assimilation, ammonification and denitrification.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 14 — Ecosystem, Section 14.5: Nutrient Cycling
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 14 — Ecosystem, Section 14.5: Nutrient Cycling

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Apomixis refers to:

options.A: Fusion of two gametes without fertilisation

questions.ncert_line: Apomixis is the production of seeds without fertilisation. It occurs by: development from diploid egg cell (diplospory), development from nucellus/integument cells (apospory), or vegetative apomixis (adventive embryony). Apomixis is common in Asteraceae and grasses.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 2 — Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants, Section 2.7.3: Apomixis
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 2 — Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants, Section 2.7.3: Apomixis

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Syphilis is caused by:
questions.ncert_line: STIs (sexually transmitted infections) include bacterial infections (gonorrhoea — Neisseria gonorrhoeae; syphilis — Treponema pallidum) and viral infections (AIDS — HIV; genital herpes — HSV-2; hepatitis B — HBV; genital warts — HPV). Trichomoniasis is caused by protozoan Trichomonas vaginalis.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 4 — Reproductive Health, Section 4.4: Sexually Transmitted Infections
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 4 — Reproductive Health, Section 4.4: Sexually Transmitted Infections

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In which ART procedure are the gametes (not the embryo) transferred into the fallopian tube?
questions.ncert_line: Assisted Reproductive Technologies include: IVF-ET (in vitro fertilisation and embryo transfer), GIFT (gamete intrafallopian transfer), ICSI (intracytoplasmic sperm injection), ZIFT (zygote intrafallopian transfer). The test tube baby procedure uses IVF and ET.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 4 — Reproductive Health, Section 4.5: Assisted Reproductive Technologies
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 4 — Reproductive Health, Section 4.5: Assisted Reproductive Technologies

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In which organism is sex determined by the ZW-ZZ system (females are ZW)?
questions.ncert_line: In humans, sex is determined by XX-XY system (male = XY). In grasshopper, male = XO (45 chromosomes). In birds, female = ZW and male = ZZ. In honey bees, males (drones) are haploid and females (workers/queen) are diploid — haplo-diploidy.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 5 — Principles of Inheritance, Section 5.7: Sex Determination
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 5 — Principles of Inheritance, Section 5.7: Sex Determination

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Genes that are close together on the same chromosome tend to be inherited together. This phenomenon is called:
questions.ncert_line: Linked genes tend to be inherited together because they are on the same chromosome. Morgan showed that genes close together on a chromosome show less crossing over (lower recombination frequency). The percentage of recombination ≈ map distance in centimorgans (cM).
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 5 — Principles of Inheritance, Section 5.8: Linkage and Crossing Over
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 5 — Principles of Inheritance, Section 5.8: Linkage and Crossing Over

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A mutation involving a change in a single base pair of DNA is called a:
questions.ncert_line: A point mutation involves a change in a single base pair. Frame-shift mutations (insertion/deletion) alter the reading frame from the point of mutation. Transition substitutes a purine for a purine (or pyrimidine for pyrimidine). Transversion substitutes a purine for a pyrimidine or vice versa.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 5 — Principles of Inheritance, Section 5.10: Mutation
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 5 — Principles of Inheritance, Section 5.10: Mutation

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In an autosomal recessive trait, two unaffected parents can produce an affected offspring. This means both parents are:
questions.ncert_line: Pedigree analysis is used to determine the pattern of inheritance of a trait. Autosomal dominant traits: appear in every generation, unaffected parents cannot have affected offspring. Autosomal recessive: can skip generations; can appear in offspring of two unaffected carriers.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 5 — Principles of Inheritance, Section 5.9: Pedigree Analysis
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 5 — Principles of Inheritance, Section 5.9: Pedigree Analysis

questions.subject: Biology
